claim: ESMAP's mini-grid market analysis identifies productive use of electricity (agriculture, commerce, light industry) as central to mini-grid commercial viability, while noting it remains underdeveloped in most Sub-Saharan African mini-grids where residential demand dominates.

source.title: ESMAP Global Facility on Mini Grids — Scaling Up Mini Grid Markets (World Bank)

method: Reviewed World Bank productive-use framework, income-impact estimates, and mini-grid survey data for Sub-Saharan Africa.
limitations: Re-sourced June 2026 after the original World Bank topic page returned HTTP 404; the specific '20-30% income increase' and 'fewer than 10% of mini-grids serve productive use' figures were not carried over and need a dedicated source.
This page describes program direction, not a peer-reviewed impact estimate.
Productive-use adoption varies widely by country and tariff design.

claim: Agricultural value-chain applications — milling, drying, cold storage, irrigation, and processing — represent the highest-impact productive-use opportunities in rural areas, but equipment adoption depends on affordability, technical capacity, and market access that electricity alone does not guarantee.

source.title: Electrification and agricultural value chains: a systematic review

method: Reviewed systematic search of agricultural electrification studies, value-chain analysis, and equipment-adoption barrier documentation.
limitations: The review combines studies from diverse agricultural contexts — value-chain-specific effects may not generalize across crops and regions.
Equipment-adoption barriers (affordability, maintenance capacity, market access) are documented qualitatively but not systematically quantified across studies.
Most studies measure equipment ownership rather than actual productive use — a farmer who owns an electric mill may still use it infrequently.
Publication bias toward positive findings cannot be excluded — electrification projects with no productive-use impact may be underreported.

claim: ESMAP's Mini Grids market outlook reports that demand on Sub-Saharan African mini-grids is dominated by residential connections, with productive-use loads a minority of demand, and that productive-use growth — a key assumption in mini-grid business models — frequently lags projections.

source.title: Mini Grids for Half a Billion People: Market Outlook and Handbook for Decision-Makers (ESMAP, World Bank)

method: Reviewed mini-grid operator survey methodology, productive-use load data, and business-model assessment across SSA countries.
limitations: Re-sourced June 2026 after the original operator-survey URL (minigridfinancingfacility.org) became unreachable; the specific 'less than 20 percent of demand' figure needs a dedicated operator-survey source.
Market-outlook figures are aggregate and vary substantially by country, operator, and tariff design.
Demand composition evolves as connections mature, so a single snapshot understates later productive-use uptake.

claim: Enterprise surveys in rural electrification areas show that electricity access increases business formation by 5-15 percent on average, but the effect is highly context-dependent — areas with existing market infrastructure and human capital benefit more than isolated rural communities.

source.title: Rural electrification and enterprise development: evidence from SSA

method: Reviewed enterprise survey methodology, business-formation effect estimates, and context-dependency analysis for rural electrification in SSA.
limitations: The 5-15 percent business-formation increase is pooled across diverse contexts — specific regions may see larger or smaller effects depending on market infrastructure.
Enterprise surveys capture formal business registration — informal productive use that does not register as a business is missed.
The context-dependency finding means that electrification in isolated communities without market access may have near-zero productive-use impact.
Follow-up periods are typically 2-5 years — long-term enterprise sustainability and scaling are rarely assessed.
